apercu: The kingdom of Urartu (Biainili in Urartian, the biblical "kingdom of Ararat") united the highlands around Lake Van under Arame, its first attested king (r. c. 860-843), soon followed by Sarduri I, who founded Tushpa as a fortified capital (r. c. 840-830) and gave his name to the continuous royal line. Assyria's great rival, it excelled in hydraulics — the Menua canal, named for a king who reigned a few decades later, over 70 km long, still irrigates the Van region —, in bronze metallurgy and in fortress architecture. King Argishti I founded the fortress of Erebuni there in 782 BC — regarded as the symbolic birth of Yerevan, though the city itself only developed in the Middle Ages — under the protection of the national god Haldi.

Weakened by Cimmerian and Scythian raids, the kingdom collapsed around 590 BC, probably under Median pressure, in circumstances still debated for lack of cuneiform sources covering this final period. It is on this substrate that the Armenian people emerges into the sources.
contexte: Permanent rivalry with Assyria: campaigns of Shalmaneser III, sack of the holy city of Musasir by Sargon II in 714 BC.

capitales.info: Capital founded by Sarduri I on a rocky spur above Lake Van. The citadel preserves the kings' cuneiform annals and the royal rock tombs.

Fortress founded in 782 BC by Argishti I on Arin Berd hill, with 6,600 people deported from Hatti and Tsupani and resettled as colonists. Yerevan descends from it symbolically, but without direct urban continuity — the city itself only re-develops in the Middle Ages.
Royal residence of Rusa II (7th c. BC) near Tushpa, the kingdom's last great foundation.

pouvoir: Median then Achaemenid empire · Orontid dynasty
apercu: After Urartu's fall the plateau passed to the Medes, then to Achaemenid Persia. The trilingual Behistun inscription (520 BC) mentions the name "Armina" for the first time.

The local Orontid (Yervanduni) dynasty, traditionally linked to a Persian dignitary named Orontas mentioned in Xenophon's Anabasis (with no firmly established connection to the satrap of Armenia), governed the satrapy, which paid 400 talents of silver according to Herodotus (Histories, III, 93) and reportedly supplied 20,000 colts a year for the feasts of Mithra according to Strabo (Geography, XI, 14, 9). Xenophon and the Ten Thousand crossed its snowy villages in 401 BC.
contexte: First imperial occupation: Armenia is a province (satrapy) integrated into the Persian system of royal roads and tribute, but administered by an Armenian dynasty.

capitales.info: The old Urartian fortress became the satrapal seat: Achaemenid silver rhytons and a columned apadana hall were found there.
Former Urartian Argishtikhinili, residence of the Orontids in the Araxes plain — future first capital of the independent kingdom.

apercu: Armenia remained nominally under Macedonian administration after Gaugamela (331 BC), but it was Alexander's death (323 BC) and the chaos of the following Diadochi wars that let satrap Orontes (Yervand) become independent in fact: Armenia was never really conquered by the Macedonians.

The Orontids reigned as kings between the Seleucids to the south and Pontus to the north, in measured Hellenisation — the rocks of Armavir bear Greek inscriptions quoting Euripides and Hesiod. Around 200 BC Antiochos III deposed the last Orontid and entrusted the country to his generals Artaxias and Zariadres — who would soon betray him.
contexte: An imperial interlude: real independence under theoretical Seleucid suzerainty, acknowledged by intermittent tribute.

capitales.info: First royal capital; Greek inscriptions carved on its rocks quote Euripides and Hesiod.
Founded by Yervand IV at the confluence of the Araxes and Akhurian, "on steep rocks", last Orontid capital.

pouvoir: Artaxiad dynasty · apogee under Tigranes II the Great
apercu: In 189 BC, after Antiochos III's defeat by Rome, Artaxias I proclaimed himself king and, according to Strabo, "gathered the lands where one and the same language was spoken, Armenian".

Apogee under Tigranes II the Great (95–55 BC): allied to Mithridates VI Eupator of Pontus, whose daughter Cleopatra he married (c. 93 BC), he invaded Cappadocia and fought the Parthians (c. 87 BC), before conquering Syria, Cilicia and Atropatene and proclaiming himself "king of kings" and founded around 83 BC his imperial capital, Tigranakert, peopled with deported Greeks. His empire stretched from the Caspian to the Mediterranean. But in 71-70 BC, Mithridates, his defeated father-in-law, took refuge at his court: Tigranes' refusal to extradite him triggered the Roman invasion — Lucullus (69) and Pompey (66 BC) reduced it to Greater Armenia.

The dynasty ended with the deposition of Queen Erato around 12 AD. Rome and Parthia then contested the throne through proxy kings for half a century (12–66 AD), until Tiridates I's founding compromise inaugurated the Arsacid dynasty.
contexte: Armenia becomes the buffer state disputed between Rome and Parthia — a role it would keep for seven centuries.

capitales.info: Founded c. 176 BC on the Araxes — by tradition on the advice of Hannibal, then a refugee at court. The Romans nicknamed it "the Armenian Carthage".
Imperial capital founded c. 83 BC by Tigranes, peopled with Greeks deported from Cappadocia. Taken and sacked by Lucullus in 69 BC.

pouvoir: Arsacid dynasty, between Rome and Persia
apercu: In AD 66 Tiridates I, brother of the Parthian king, received the crown of Armenia from Nero in Rome: a founding compromise — Parthian dynasty, Roman investiture.

In 114, Emperor Trajan invaded Armenia, deposed King Parthamasiris and annexed it as a Roman province — the only time in its history — just as the Roman Empire reached its greatest territorial extent (116). On Trajan's death (August 117), his successor Hadrian, wishing to pull the Empire back to defensible borders, abandoned the province by 117-118 and restored a client kingdom.

In 224, the Sasanian Ardashir I overthrew the Parthian Arsacids: Arsacid Armenia, the dynasty's sole surviving branch, became a prime target of Sasanian hostility. Around 252, according to the tradition of Agathangelos, King Khosrov II was assassinated by the Parthian noble Anak on the orders of the Sasanian Shapur I, who then imposed decades of Persian domination — a founding episode of this same account's family vendetta that would later drive his son Tiridates III to reclaim the throne. According to the hagiographic account of Agathangelos — religious tradition rather than strictly established history —, Anak's entire family was exterminated in reprisal, except his infant son Gregory, raised Christian in exile; back in Armenia, this son of the regicide was unmasked and thrown into the pit of Khor Virap, where he survived thirteen years, before Tiridates III, struck by madness, was healed by him and converted. Traditionally dated to 301 — though some modern historians propose a later date, around 314 —, the conversion of this same Tiridates III by St Gregory the Illuminator — who became the first catholicos, founder of a lineage that would lead the Church for a century — made Christianity the state religion: a world first either way. In 387, Rome and Sasanian Persia partitioned the kingdom — about a fifth in the west for Rome, the rest under Persian sway. In 405 the monk Mesrop Mashtots created the Armenian alphabet, still in use. The monarchy was abolished by Persia in 428, at the nakharars' own request.
contexte: Partition of 387: Rome and Sasanian Persia divided the kingdom — about one fifth in the west for Rome, the rest under Persian influence.

capitales.info: Capital in the 3rd century and spiritual seat: Etchmiadzin cathedral, founded 301–303, is among the oldest in the Christian world.
Former Artaxiad capital, still a great merchant city; a Roman-Persian treaty regulated the silk trade there.
Founded c. 335 by Khosrov III; the late political capital, later seat of the catholicoi and of Persian and Arab governors.

apercu: In the east, Sasanian Persia governed Persarmenia through a marzpan (viceroy) seated at Dvin; in the west, Emperor Justinian I reorganised his Armenian provinces (Armenia I–IV) in 536 through Novel 31, replacing the nakharars' hereditary authority with Roman law and justifying the change by calling local inheritance customs "barbaric" — an Armenian revolt broke out as early as 538-539 in response. The Byzantine general Narses, of Armenian origin, served Justinian until his defeat of the Ostrogoths in Italy (552).

In 451, the Persian attempt to impose Zoroastrianism provoked the battle of Avarayr (26 May): the sparapet Vardan Mamikonian, the kingdom's supreme military commander, died in battle against a larger Sasanian army, according to Armenian sources — a military defeat, but a moral victory that the Church canonised (the feast of Vartanank'). A generation later, his nephew Vahan Mamikonian took up arms again in the 480s: his revolt led to the treaty of Nvarsak (484), which finally guaranteed the religious freedom Vardan had died for.

In 591, Byzantine emperor Maurice restored the young Chosroes II, driven out by a usurper, to the Persian throne, in exchange for the frontier moving east in Byzantium's favour. This same Chosroes II took revenge on his benefactor: in 602 Emperor Maurice was overthrown and executed by the usurper Phocas — Chosroes II invoked this murder as a casus belli, took the Byzantine fortress of Dara after a long siege in 604, and recaptured Dvin, barely restored in 591: Armenia then served as a battlefield and logistical base for Emperor Heraclius's campaigns across the Caucasus (624-625). The peace of 628, after Chosroes II's fall, brought Armenia back under Byzantine influence — a fragile, short-lived reunification, swept away by 640 with the first Arab raids.
contexte: A kingless age: real power lay with the great nakharar families and the Church, which broke with Constantinople at the council of Dvin (554).

capitales.info: Seat of the marzpan and, from 485, of the catholicosate. A great caravan city, repeatedly destroyed by earthquakes and sieges.
Byzantine stronghold on the invasion road, founded in the 5th century — today's Erzurum.

apercu: The first Arab raids reached Dvin as early as 640. Around 652, prince Theodoros Rshtuni negotiated an autonomous tributary status for Armenia with Mu'awiya — a more structuring agreement than the initial raids, though Armeno-Arab allegiance then shifted back and forth several times before the caliphate organised the province of Arminiya around 700 — Armenia, Georgian Iberia and Caucasian Albania — governed by an ostikan from Dvin, then Bardaa. In 705, to break the nobility, the Arabs, according to tradition, burned several hundred nakharars alive in the churches of Nakhichevan and Khram.

The nakharar revolts (703, 748, 774–775 — crushed at Bagrevand — then 851-852, the direct prelude to Bugha's crackdown) bled the old Mamikonian and Kamsarakan houses, weakened militarily and demographically — to the benefit of the Bagratids, who had stayed clear of these uprisings and reaped the political dividend: Ashot Msaker received the caliphate's title of "prince of Armenia" (ishkhan) around 804-806. The Bagratid path was not straightforward, though: between 852 and 855, Abbasid general Bugha al-Kabir led a fresh crackdown, deporting several Armenian princes, including Bagratids, and the catholicos himself, to Samarra. The dynasty recovered: its descendants secured the higher title of "prince of princes" before reclaiming the crown in 884-885 depending on the source (caliphal recognition usually dated to 885).
contexte: Occupation by integration: tribute and Arab garrisons, but the Christian princes' autonomy was recognised by treaty (Muawiya's pact, 652).

capitales.info: Seat of the ostikan, with mosque, customs and mints striking "Arminiya" dirhams. The city remained the catholicos' seat too.

apercu: In 884-885 (depending on the source), Ashot I Bagratuni was crowned "king of the Armenians", recognised by both caliph and Byzantine emperor. His grandson Ashot II "the Iron" received from Abbasid caliph al-Muqtadir, around 914-922, the higher title of "king of kings" (Shahanshah), in reward for his victories over local emirs. Ani became the capital in 961: the "city of 1001 churches", a Silk Road stage of perhaps 100,000 inhabitants. In Constantinople, Emperor Nikephoros II Phokas (963-969) annexed the Armenian kingdom of Taron around 966-968; his successor John I Tzimiskes (969-976), from the Armenian Kourkouas family (Basil I's own Armenian origin, founder of the Macedonian dynasty, is a separate and disputed historiographical question) — then conducted his Syrian campaigns with the support of the Bagratids of Ashot III.

The kingdom split into sister realms — Vaspurakan (908), Kars (963), Lori (978), Syunik (987) — easy prey: in 1021, King Senekerim Artsruni of Vaspurakan, threatened by the first Turkish raids, ceded his kingdom to Byzantine emperor Basil II in exchange for lands in Cappadocia — a negotiated cession, not a conquest, occurring just as Basil II campaigned in the Caucasus against Georgia (1021-1022). Byzantium then annexed Ani (1045) through a far murkier mechanism: Emperor Constantine IX lured the last king Gagik II to Constantinople on the pretext of negotiations, then detained him there until he surrendered his capital in exchange for lands in Cappadocia — dispossession disguised as an honorific compensation, without even a prior military defeat; the Seljuks sacked Ani in 1064 and crushed Byzantium at Manzikert (1071), opening Anatolia to the Turks.
contexte: A golden age of architecture (Ani, Aghtamar, Sanahin, Haghpat) against a background of fatal political fragmentation.

capitales.info: Capital from 961, between ravines and double walls. Its cathedral (1001) is the work of Trdat — the architect called to repair the dome of Hagia Sophia.
Island palace of king Gagik Artsruni: the church of the Holy Cross (915–921) and its biblical reliefs are masterpieces of medieval art.
Bagratid capital from 928 to 961, then seat of the kingdom of Vanand until its cession to Byzantium.

pouvoir: Armenian Kingdom of Cilicia · Zakarid principality · Mongols
apercu: Fleeing the Seljuk invasions that followed the fall of Ani (1064) and the Byzantine defeat at Manzikert (1071), Armenian princes settled in Cilicia on the Mediterranean: the Rubenid principality (1080) became a kingdom when Leo I the Magnificent was crowned in 1198. Allied to the Crusaders and then to the Mongols, the state prospered through its ports — Ayas, described by Marco Polo.

On the highlands, the Zakarian brothers, generals of Queen Tamar of Georgia, liberated the north (1199–1236) before the Mongol wave. Hetoum I submitted to the Mongols as early as 1246 and sealed the alliance with his journey to Karakorum (1254); but in 1266 the Mamluks under Baibars invaded Cilicia, sacking Sis and Adana and killing in battle Prince Toros, Hetoum I's eldest son, at the battle of Mari, where his younger brother Leo (the future Leo II) was captured and later released — the kingdom's isolation deepened with the fall of Antioch, its last crusader ally, in 1268. The decline of the Mongol Ilkhanate after 1335 left Cilicia without its protector: the strategic port of Ayas was lost as early as 1347, and Sis, the shrunken capital, fell to the Mamluks in 1375 after a month-long siege. Captured, the last king Leo V of Lusignan was imprisoned in Cairo, then freed in 1382 through Castilian-Aragonese mediation; he wandered Europe before dying in exile in Paris in 1393 — his tomb is at Saint-Denis. With the fall of Sis the last independent Armenian state died out: none would rise again until 1918.
contexte: Armenia now existed in two places: a maritime kingdom in Cilicia, and a highland passed from the Seljuks to the Mongols (1236) and then to Turkmen hordes.

capitales.info: Capital of the kingdom of Cilicia and seat of a catholicosate that remained until 1930. Taken by the Mamluks in 1375 after a long siege.
A merchant renaissance under the Zakarians, then slow decline under the Mongols; an earthquake (1319) and shifting trade routes finished the city.

pouvoir: Ottoman Empire (west) · Safavid then Qajar Persia (east)
apercu: After the battle of Chaldiran (1514, under Selim I) Ottomans and Safavids fought over the plateau. Suleiman the Magnificent and Safavid shah Tahmasp I sealed the peace of Amasya (1555), which drew a first lasting border — Erzurum and the west to the Ottomans, Erivan and the east to the Persians — a border repeatedly upended in the interval — notably by Shah Abbas I's campaigns, which retook Erivan (1604) and then much of the Caucasus from the Ottomans (up to 1618) — before the peace of Zuhab (1639) restored a line close to that of 1555, 84 years later. In 1604 Shah Abbas deported hundreds of thousands of Armenians to Isfahan — New Julfa, which would dominate the silk trade as far as India.

After a fresh Ottoman occupation of the Caucasus (1724), Nader Shah retook Erivan in 1735 and restored Persian sovereignty; his heavy war taxation, to fund his campaigns as far as India (1739), weighed heavily on the Armenian provinces. His assassination in 1747 fragmented Persia: Caucasus governors became near-independent khans — the khanate of Erivan consolidated then, while Panah Ali Khan, founder of the Karabakh khanate, built the fortress of Shusha around 1751-1752 as his new capital — his son Ibrahim Khalil Khan succeeded him around 1759-1763 (exact date debated among sources).

The Russo-Persian War of 1826-1828, triggered by Shah Fath Ali Shah seeking to recover territories lost in 1813, ended in Persian defeat: the treaty of Turkmenchay (10 February 1828), negotiated on the Russian side by General Ivan Paskevich with diplomatic support from secretary-playwright Alexander Griboyedov — killed the following year in Tehran —, ceded the khanates of Erivan and Nakhichevan to Russia. On 21 March 1828, an imperial decree merged them into the « Armenian Oblast », the first modern territory to officially bear the name "Armenia" under Russian sovereignty; the treaty also allowed Armenians from Persia to emigrate, some 40,000 to 45,000 of whom settled in Eastern Armenia. The territorial matrix of modern Armenia, from which would come the First Republic (1918) and independent Armenia (1991).
contexte: Four centuries without a state: the Church (Etchmiadzin, restored 1441) and the merchant diasporas became the nation's backbone.

capitales.info: Seat of the Persian khanate, a city of fortresses, mosques and gardens on the Hrazdan. Its capture in 1827 earned Paskevich the title "Count of Erivan".
The Holy See, reinstalled in 1441, the only pan-Armenian institution: its encyclicals and collections linked Constantinople, Isfahan, Venice and Madras.
